gist: Blockworks analysts dissect Solana's broken value-accrual story and two live fixes (SIMD-553's compute-unit burn and Helius's proposal to double the disinflation rate), argue burn only offsets ~12% of issuance and is gated behind an undated Alpenglow upgrade, and frame DeFi smart-contract risk as rising sharply as AI models become better exploiters than patchers. They also flag consecutive Bitcoin ETF outflows, Saylor's circular bid, and a structural decorrelation of cash-flow-generating tokens from BTC.

themes.theme: Solana value accrual: SIMD-553 compute-unit burn

insights.claim: SIMD-553 (formerly SIMD-547, proposed by KB of Temporal) would restore an activity-linked SOL burn via a new fee tied to requested compute units rather than priority/base fees, set at 0.5 lamports per CU, burning roughly 7,000-9,000 SOL per day at current usage.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: A live, concrete mechanism to redirect application activity value back to SOL holders; watch the lamport parameter (0.1/0.2 vs 0.5) and the GitHub vote as the key valuation catalyst.

insights.claim: At ~8,000 SOL/day the SIMD-553 burn equals about $190M annually and offsets only ~12% of daily SOL issuance, so it makes SOL less inflationary but not disinflationary at current levels.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Tempers the bull case: the burn is an offset, not a structural supply sink, so SOL value accrual still hinges on the separate issuance-reduction track.
insights.quote: If you assume like 8,000 sole burn uh based on current estimates that that's around $190 million in burn, but you are right that that currently doesn't necessarily make sole disinflationary.

insights.claim: SIMD-553's burn scales with both network usage and network capacity, so as Solana raises block CU limits and shortens slot times the burn rises roughly linearly, aligning the network's scaling roadmap with SOL's economic value.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Reframes Solana's throughput ambitions (200M-1B CUs, sub-100ms blocks) as directly accretive to SOL rather than dilutive; a cleaner long-term bull thesis if it ships.

insights.claim: Pump.fun alone accounts for over 20% of Solana's compute-unit usage, and the ratio of application revenue to REV has risen to ~5x today from ~1x in 2023, showing apps capture value while SOL holders do not.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Quantifies the value leakage SIMD-553 targets; the true gap is even wider since not all REV accrues to holders, underscoring the upside if the burn captures app activity.
insights.quote: the ratio of application revenue to REB is like 5x today up from like 1x at some point 19 2023

insights.claim: SIMD-553 is viable only after the Alpenglow upgrade (which has no live timeline) because implementing it earlier would sharply raise validator voting costs and hurt smaller validators.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: The main execution risk: value-accrual fixes could be 18+ months out, draining near-term catalyst value; track Alpenglow timeline as the gating event.
insights.quote: a lot of this keeps kind of seeming like kind of theoretical in the sense that uh you can talk about like fixing value accrual, but if you always have like these dependencies

insights.claim: Brandon Watt of Anza reviewed SIMD-553 on GitHub and was broadly in favor, with implementation concerns mainly around whether the 0.5 lamport/CU parameter is too aggressive versus a 0.1-0.2 start that would spare high-frequency traders and market makers.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Core-dev sentiment is supportive, lowering political risk; the open debate is calibration, not direction.

insights.claim: Pricing-insensitive Solana users persist despite high fees: Benedict Brady's research showed Axiom traders being charged ~100x the actual Solana transaction fee yet continuing to trade there, evidencing capturable value the network currently gives away.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Supports the case that a CU burn can extract real value from sticky high-intent traders rather than driving them off-chain.

insights.claim: An EIP-1559-style CU fee would likely trigger a reaction function where protocols optimize/de-bloat their requested compute units, partly muting the burn, but tighter packing then fits more transactions per block so HFT activity refills the freed capacity.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research) and Toma
insights.so_what: Net burn impact is harder to model than headline estimates; near-term burn may undershoot as code is optimized before throughput growth catches up.

themes.theme: SOL issuance reduction and tokenomics overhang

insights.claim: Helius is proposing to double Solana's disinflation rate from 15% to 30% so SOL reaches its 1.5% terminal inflation rate in ~3 years instead of ~6, attacking the issuance side that the burn alone cannot fix.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: The faster disinflation track matters more than the burn for SOL supply dynamics; current annualized inflation is ~3.8% vs 1.5% terminal.

insights.claim: Solana today relies almost entirely on issuance for staking yield rather than activity-derived value, because SIMD-96 removed the priority-fee burn and there is no in-protocol way (absent SIMD-123) to share priority fees with stakers.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Explains the structural reason SOL staking yield is dilution, not real cash flow, and why the new SIMDs are needed; key context for SOL holders evaluating real vs nominal yield.

insights.claim: SOL's inflation problem is visible in market cap: SOL made an all-time high near $260 in January with a market cap already roughly double its 2021 peak, meaning price had to climb much further just to match prior valuation.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: A clean way to see dilution drag on price; argues SOL needs supply reform to translate adoption into per-token appreciation.

insights.claim: SOL faces a denomination risk as major apps migrate fees away from SOL toward stablecoins (Pump.fun bonding in USDC likely via a Circle deal, 0x Edge stablecoin-denominated, Jito's JTX pairs likely USDC), which a CU-based burn neutralizes because it charges in SOL regardless of fee denomination.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research) and Carlos
insights.so_what: Stablecoin-denominated app economics are a real threat to SOL demand; SIMD-553's CU basis is a deliberate hedge against that drift.

themes.theme: AI models and DeFi smart-contract security risk

insights.claim: Frontier AI coding models are currently better at exploiting smart contracts than at patching them, structurally raising tail risk for on-chain capital and favoring large, battle-tested protocols.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Implies a flight-to-quality in lending/vaults; A-tier protocols with deep security resources should keep gaining share and command relative premium over time.
insights.quote: for whatever reason like the models they're just like way better at exploiting than like uh finding patches

insights.claim: Newly released AI coding models (referred to in-episode as Anthropic's Claude Fable, the restricted version of an unreleased Mythos) showed a step-change in one-shot ability to find and fix smart-contract bugs, with both crypto developers and Toly reporting it effectively hardened code.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Capability inflection cuts both ways for crypto: better defense for serious teams, but a more dangerous toolkit if leaked to attackers; monitor model-driven exploit cadence.

insights.claim: The Zcash bug and a recent Ambient Finance hack illustrate that AI-assisted exploits are targeting stale, low-usage capital, concentrated on Ethereum where large dormant balances sit in older contracts.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Idle TVL in second-tier protocols is the soft target; investors should treat dormant-contract exposure as elevated risk regardless of headline yield.
insights.quote: even that Zcash bug that was found was like Opus 48, right?

insights.claim: Per Tarun on The Chopping Block, attacker-defender dynamics have a tipping point where every exploit on a contract hardens the rest, so the industry trends toward greater resiliency after a painful adjustment period.
insights.speaker: Toma
insights.so_what: Frames near-term exploit wave as a transient cost preceding a more robust DeFi base; positions survivors to compound trust.
insights.quote: every like attack on a random smart contract hardens the rest

insights.claim: Wallets such as Phantom are the highest-impact attack surface because they are the gateway to many protocols, making a wallet compromise far more damaging than an obscure single-protocol hack.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Concentration risk sits at the wallet layer; security premium and scrutiny should center on wallet infrastructure, not just individual dApps.

insights.claim: Heightened AI-driven exploit risk should compress valuation premiums mainly for new yield protocols while letting top teams steal premium via dispersion, though panelists report private valuations are not yet egregiously discounted.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Expect widening valuation dispersion between A-tier and long-tail protocols rather than a blanket de-rating; favor incumbents.

insights.claim: DeFi yield is shifting from fake token-emission yield toward real yield from actual risk-taking, with real-asset issuers (e.g., Ethena) and large asset managers bringing native products on-chain instead of using existing DeFi protocols as distribution.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Signals disintermediation risk for legacy DeFi rails as TradFi issuers go direct on-chain; watch which protocols remain the distribution layer.

themes.theme: MicroStrategy, Saylor and the stretch product

insights.claim: MicroStrategy funded fresh Bitcoin purchases by issuing more MSTR through its ATM after selling ~32 BTC, signaling Saylor can keep the machine running but exposing his trilemma between BTC sales, stretch dividends, and equity issuance.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: ATM issuance is the release valve; watch whether stretch's size eventually forces material BTC sales, a downstream risk for the whole market.

insights.claim: The stretch product grew large enough that a high single-digit percent was tokenized on-chain, and it has not re-pegged, so if it trades below $100 the dividend must rise, pressuring Saylor to sacrifice MSTR common shares to defend it in a worst case.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: A de-pegged stretch is a self-reinforcing drain; equity dilution to fund the dividend is the likely pressure-release, bearish for MSTR holders.

insights.claim: MicroStrategy's Bitcoin thesis has become circular: Saylor's flows grew so dominant that the market thesis on BTC is now 'Saylor is buying' rather than Bitcoin's first-principles non-sovereign-money case.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Makes BTC's marginal bid fragile and reflexive; if Saylor's buying slows, the narrative support unwinds.
insights.quote: it what started as like uh first principles or fundamentals driven thesis is now a thesis on the basis that Saylor is buying

insights.claim: MicroStrategy holds roughly 883,000 BTC (approaching one million) under an accumulate-at-any-cost strategy, large enough that its actions create material downstream impacts on other crypto assets.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Even investors ignoring MSTR should monitor it as a systemic flow risk for the broader crypto market.

themes.theme: Bitcoin market structure and crypto decorrelation

insights.claim: Bitcoin ETFs have seen over $5 billion of net outflows across five consecutive weeks, signaling institutional risk-off, while the marginal bid is mainly Saylor plus leveraged dip-buyers rather than spot demand.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Weak, leverage-driven demand structure raises drawdown risk; treat ETF flows as the cleaner institutional sentiment gauge.
insights.quote: I think it's about over 5 billion dollars in that outflows from the ETF vehicles, which to me it's more of a signal from like the institutional investors on the on the state of the market.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's realized price sits around $53,000 with spot near $60,000, and historically BTC bottoms around or below the realized price, suggesting potential consolidation toward the low-$50Ks if drawdowns continue.
insights.speaker: Carlos
insights.so_what: Gives a chart-able downside reference level (~$53K realized price) for sizing dip-buys or stops.

insights.claim: A select group of cash-flow-generating crypto assets has temporarily decorrelated from Bitcoin in 2026, reversing 2024 when most funds underperformed BTC, though panelists doubt the decorrelation can hold in USD terms if BTC keeps underperforming macro assets.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Supports rotating from BTC into fundamentally sound, revenue-generating tokens, but with awareness that a broad BTC sell-off likely drags everything down.

insights.claim: Even revenue-generating crypto businesses remain levered to trading activity, and since BTC is the most-traded asset by volume, a wide BTC sell-off would still cut their revenue estimates despite fundamental decorrelation.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Caps the safety of 'fundamental' tokens in a downturn; growth in RWAs and tokenized equities could reduce this BTC-volume dependence over time.

insights.claim: Per Austin Barrack, this is not the market to hide in BTC, reflecting a regime shift where improving regulatory clarity lets fundamentally sound tokens pass cash flow to holders rather than everyone defaulting to Bitcoin as the safe haven.
insights.speaker: Ian (Cairo Street Research)
insights.so_what: Challenges the reflexive BTC-as-safety trade; argues for selectively owning cash-flow tokens over passive BTC exposure.
insights.quote: Austin Barrack had a tweet like maybe 3 weeks ago saying that this isn't the market to be hiding uh in BTC

themes.theme: AI model access and the Fable/Mythos tiering

insights.claim: Anthropic released Claude Fable as a publicly available, restricted version of a more powerful unreleased model (Mythos), with hardcoded safeguards that refuse or silently downgrade to a prior model on sensitive tasks like cybersecurity and frontier-model development.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Capability tiering and white-listing limits the worst-case proliferation of AI exploit tools, partially capping the systemic DeFi attack risk the panel feared.
insights.quote: We don't get full-fledged Mythos. We're in the permanent underclass, so we get fed Fable instead.

insights.claim: Venice sold off hard, falling from roughly $1,850 to about $1,350 in a few days, possibly because users seeking private access to Mythos-class models reacted to getting a handicapped version, or simply from macro de-risking around the Iran conflict.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Decentralized/private-AI tokens are sensitive to model-access policy; the cause is unresolved, a watch item for the AI-crypto basket.
